messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist selecting replacement self-report items for a PTSD patient.

Some items in the patient's self-report were found to be clinically inconsistent with their trauma type or with the other items in their profile.
Your job: select one replacement per flagged item from the pool below.

SELECTION CRITERIA — in order of priority:
1. Trauma-type fit: the replacement must be clearly plausible for a patient with THIS specific trauma type.
   Ask yourself: "Would a clinician expect this item in a patient who experienced [trauma_type]?"
   If the answer is uncertain, pick a different item.
2. Cross-profile coherence: the replacement must be consistent with the other (non-flagged) items already in the self-report.
   Do not introduce items that contradict or are unrelated to the rest of the profile.
3. Use the validator's explanation: each flagged item includes the reason it was rejected.
   The replacement should directly address that reason.

Available replacement items (only for flagged components):
  Memory:
    - Visual Memories: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
    - Nonverbal Memory: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
    - Auditory Recall: When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
    - Reliving Event: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
    - Olfactory Recall: When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened.
    - Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
    - Snapshot Memory: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
    - Memory Replay: My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
    - Vivid Memory: My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
    - Photographic Recall: Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
    - Bodily Reenactment: When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
    - More Real Than Normal: My memories of the event feel more real and vivid than memories of ordinary things
    - Full Sensory Reliving: When the memory comes back it feels completely real across all my senses at once
    - Pain Recall: When the memory returns I feel physical pain in the same parts of my body as during the event
    - Taste Vividness: I can taste what I tasted during the event when the memory returns
    - Feels Present Not Past: The memory feels like it is happening right now rather than something that is over
    - Fragmented Pieces: My memories of the event come in disconnected pieces that do not fit together
    - Memory Gaps: There are parts of the event that I simply cannot remember no matter how hard I try
    - Confused Sequence: I cannot put the memories of the event in the right order
    - Partial Clarity: Some moments of the event are extremely clear while others are completely blank
    - Incomplete Picture: I feel like I am only remembering parts of what happened and the rest is missing
    - Sudden Pieces: Fragments of the memory appear suddenly without warning and then disappear
    - No Coherent Story: I cannot tell a clear story of what happened even to myself
    - Cannot Find Words: I know what happened but I am unable to find words to describe it
    - Story Feels Unreal: The memory is so extreme it is hard to believe it actually happened to me
    - Cannot Explain Clearly: When I try to explain what happened I lose the thread and cannot finish
    - Memory Feels Alien: The memory feels like it belongs to someone else rather than to me
    - Overwhelm on Recall: When the memory returns I feel so overwhelmed by emotion that I have to stop
    - Terror on Recall: Remembering the event brings back the same terror I felt at the time
    - Shame on Recall: When I remember the event I feel intense shame
    - Guilt on Recall: When I remember the event I feel intense guilt
    - Rage on Recall: When I remember the event I feel intense rage
    - Grief on Recall: When I remember the event I feel overwhelming grief
    - Emotion Without Image: Sometimes I feel the full emotional intensity of the event without any clear image or memory
    - Emotionless Recall: Sometimes I can recall what happened but feel completely numb, as if it happened to a stranger
    - Uncontrolled Access: The memory forces its way into my mind when I do not want it to
    - Triggered Without Warning: Something in my surroundings can instantly bring back the full memory without warning
    - Hard to Recall Deliberately: When I try to deliberately remember what happened I find it difficult to access the memory
    - Blocked Before Complete: I find myself pushing the memory away before I can fully recall it
  Negative Appraisals:
    - Self-Blame: The event happened because of the way I acted
    - Self-Doubt: I can't trust that I will do the right thing
    - Weakness Belief: I am a weak person
    - Fear of Rage: I will not be able to control my anger and will do something terrible
    - Low Coping: I can't deal with even the slightest upset
    - Lost Happiness: I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.
    - Mistrust: People can't be trusted
    - Hypervigilance: I have to be on guard all the time
    - Emotional Numbness: I feel dead inside
    - Fear of Harm: You can never know who will harm you
    - Constant Vigilance: I have to be especially careful because you never know what can happen next
    - Inadequacy: I am inadequate
    - Thought Avoidance: If I think about the event, I will not be able to handle it
    - Character Blame: The event happened to me because of the sort of person I am
    - Fear Going Crazy: My reactions since the event mean that I am going crazy
    - Lost Emotions: I will never be able to feel normal emotions again
    - World is Dangerous: The world is a dangerous place
    - Others-Would-Cope: Somebody else would have stopped the event from happening
    - Permanent Damage: I have permanently changed for the worse
    - Dehumanization: I feel like an object, not like a person
    - Self-Comparison: Somebody else would not have gotten into this situation
    - Can't Rely on Others: I can't rely on other people
    - Isolation: I feel isolated and set apart from others
    - Hopelessness: I have no future
    - Helplessness: I can't stop bad things from happening to me
    - Distrust Others: People are not what they seem
    - Life Destroyed: My life has been destroyed by the trauma
    - Defective Self: There is something wrong with me as a person
    - Bad Coper: My reactions since the event show that I am a lousy coper
    - Intrinsic Fault: There is something about me that made the event happen
    - Identity Loss: I feel like I don't know myself anymore
    - Can't Rely on Self: I can't rely on myself
    - No Good Ahead: Nothing good can happen to me anymore
    - Body Shame: I feel ashamed of what happened to my body
    - Contamination: I feel dirty or contaminated because of what happened
    - Humiliation: I feel permanently humiliated by what happened
    - Exposed to Others: I feel that others can see what happened to me just by looking at me
    - Survival Guilt: I feel guilty for surviving when others did not
    - Failed to Protect: I should have protected others and I failed
    - Violated Values: I acted in ways that go against who I am
    - Inaction Guilt: I will never forgive myself for not doing more to stop what happened
    - Betrayal: I was betrayed by someone I trusted
    - Injustice: What happened to me was deeply unfair and nothing has been done about it
    - No Accountability: The people responsible have faced no consequences
    - Institutional Failure: The people or institutions that should have protected me let me down
    - Body Unreliable: I can no longer trust my own body
    - Body as Enemy: My body keeps reacting as if I am still in danger even when I am safe
    - Physical Vulnerability: My body has been permanently damaged by what happened
    - Lost Meaning: Nothing in my life feels meaningful anymore
    - Shattered Beliefs: My beliefs about how the world works have been completely destroyed
    - Lost Faith: I have lost my faith in people, life, or a higher power
    - Life Not Worth Living: I am not sure my life is worth living anymore
    - Belong Nowhere: I no longer belong anywhere
    - Lost Community: I have lost my connection to my community and culture
    - Outsider: I am permanently an outsider now
    - Failed Role: I have failed in my most important role in life
    - No Longer Capable: I am no longer capable of doing the things that defined me
    - Unrecognisable Self: The person I have become is unrecognisable to me

Patient demographics:
  age: 58
  gender: Male
  ethnicity: South Asian
  relationship_status: Married
  occupation: Plumber
  trauma_type: Terrorism
  pcl5: 80

Current self-report (items NOT flagged must remain unchanged):
  Triggers: Nighttime/Dark: Night-time or waking up in the dark, Auditory (Explosion): Explosion or bang sounds, Loss of Control: Lack of control
  Threat: Scanning Exits: I scan for exits as soon as I enter any room or building, Threat in Faces: I study people's faces and body language to detect if they are a threat, Alert at Home: I feel on guard even inside my own home
  Memory: Snapshot Memory: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind., Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily., Story Feels Unreal: The memory is so extreme it is hard to believe it actually happened to me
  Negative Appraisals: Mistrust: People can't be trusted, Isolation: I feel isolated and set apart from others, Outsider: I am permanently an outsider now

Flagged items with reasons for rejection:
  Memory / Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.: This conflicts with the severe PTSD profile and with the other memory items, which describe distressing, intrusive, fragmented trauma memory. Very easy verbal access to the trauma is hard to reconcile with a PCL-5 of 80 and the rest of the formulation.
  Memory / Story Feels Unreal: The memory is so extreme it is hard to believe it actually happened to me: This can fit trauma-related dissociation, but it sits awkwardly with 'I can talk about what happened very easily' within the same memory node. Together they suggest opposing memory states without a clear clinical bridge.
  Negative Appraisals / Mistrust: People can't be trusted: This is somewhat broad, but it is clinically coherent with terrorism-related trauma and the high-threat formulation. No issue.

For each flagged item, select ONE replacement key from the pool above.
The replacement must fit the patient's trauma type and be coherent with their other self-report items.
IMPORTANT: Do not re-select any of the flagged item keys. Each replacement must be a different key.
{'Memory / Verbal Access': ['No Coherent Story'], 'Memory / Story Feels Unreal': ['Fragmented Pieces'], 'Negative Appraisals / Mistrust': ['Distrust Others']}
